generations.text:
My Dearest Eliza,

If you are reading these words, then I must be gone, or you have chosen to ignore the one who once held you in his arms, who once whispered lullabies to you as you slept beneath the sea of stars, who once watched over you with more love than words could ever express. But if you are still out there, still breathing, still living your life, then I beg you—come home.

It has been five years since we last spoke, and I have not written, for I feared the words would be too heavy, too much to bear. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars in fury, I find myself unable to hold back. I am not the man I once was, and I fear I may not live to see the dawn.

You were but a child when you left, and I was a fool to let you go. I told myself it was for your own good, that you would find your own path, your own destiny, and that I could not be the father you needed. But I was wrong, Eliza. I was wrong to believe that the world would treat you kindly, that the world would not try to break you. I was wrong to think that distance would heal the wounds I could not mend.

I have watched the sea for years, and I have seen the storms. I have seen the tempests

**April 12th, 1887**  
**St. Agnes Lighthouse, Isle of Wight**  

My Dearest Eleanor,  

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that feels as heavy as the storm that now brews on the horizon. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very land whole. I have been here for thirty years, and never before have I felt such a sense of dread. If the heavens are to be trusted, I shall not see dawn. But I must write, for I fear that if I do not, the silence will be louder than the storm.  

You have not spoken to me in five years, Eleanor. Five years of silence, of letters unopened, of the cruel indifference that has turned our home into a tomb. I have watched the years pass, and in their wake, I have watched your name fade from my memory, like the light that dims at the end of a long night. I have watched the lantern burn, and I have watched the sea rise, but never have I watched the heart of a father wither so slowly.  

I do not know what drove you away. I do not know if it was the cruel words I spoke, or the silence I kept, or the love that was never enough. But I do know this: I have loved you with a love that has never wavered, even when you

**Dear Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it will find you far from where you should be—perhaps in the comfort of a warm hearth, surrounded by the laughter of friends and the soft glow of candlelight. But I write to you not from the safety of my home, but from the edge of the cliff where the sea howls and the wind screams like a banshee. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take more than just my life.

It has been five long years since the last letter you sent me, and I have waited, hoping for your return. I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years now, and though the work is demanding, it has given me a sense of purpose. But the loneliness has crept in, like the tide at low water, and I have often wondered if you still remember me at all. I have tried to write to you, but my hands have trembled, and my heart has been too heavy to put pen to paper.

Tonight, the clouds have gathered like a storm on the horizon. The sea, once calm and gentle, now roars with fury, and the waves crash against the rocks with a ferocity that is both beautiful and terrifying. I have lit the lamp, and the light dances across the waves, a beacon for the lost and the weary. But I wonder if it will be enough to guide you back to me, or if it will

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the wails of the sea, and the sky dark as the depths of the abyss. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may claim me before I can see the dawn. Yet, as I sit here by the great lantern, its light flickering like a dying ember, I find myself compelled to write—though I know you may never read these words.

Five years have passed since you left, and five years have been a cruel silence between us. I have not spoken your name in all that time, nor have I dared to think of you, save in the quiet hours when the world is still and the sea whispers of things best left forgotten. But tonight, as the tempest gathers strength, I find myself unable to hold back the truth.

You were once the light of my life, Eleanor. The little girl who would climb the stairs to the lantern room with her nightgown tucked under her arm, her eyes wide with wonder at the stars. You would press your ear to the glass and tell me of the constellations, as if they were old friends. I would smile, and the world would seem brighter, more full of promise.

But time, as it often does, has a way of changing things. The world moved on, and so did you. You left, not with words, but with a silence that echoed louder than any argument. I did

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health, though I dares not presume upon the kindness of fate. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and though I have tried to write, the words have always seemed to fall short of the truth I wished to convey. Tonight, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, I find myself compelled to reach out once more.

The sea has always been my constant companion, a force both terrible and beautiful, and I have spent my life in its service. But I fear the sea may soon claim me, and I have no wish to leave this world without one final word to you.

I have often thought of you, Eleanor. Of the days we shared in our youth, when the world seemed full of possibility and your laughter echoed through the halls of our home. I remember how you used to stand by the window, watching the waves with such wonder, and how you once asked me if the sea ever spoke. I told you it did, in the language of the wind and the foam, and I hope you still remember that.

I know I have been a poor father, and I regret the distance that has grown between us. I have not been the man I wished to be, and I fear that my silence has done more harm than good. But I have no regrets in my service to the light, for it has given me purpose, and in that purpose, I have found a measure

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in the same silence that has surrounded us for so many years. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I have often wondered if you still remember the sound of my voice—or if it has faded like the light of the lighthouse on a stormy night.

Tonight, the winds howl like a wounded beast, and the sea roars with fury. The sky is painted in shades of black and grey, and the air is thick with the promise of chaos. I have been watching the horizon for hours, my heart heavy with both the weight of the storm and the burden of unspoken words. I know the sea has always been my companion, but tonight, it feels as though it is calling me to its depths, and I am not sure if I will return.

You were always so quick to leave, Eleanor. I remember the day you left, the way your eyes grew distant as you walked out of our home, not looking back. I tried to understand, to see the pain behind your silence, but it felt like a wall had been built between us, one that neither of us could break. I thought perhaps you had found your own path, and I would follow yours with pride. But now, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself questioning whether I have been too quick to let go.

The lighthouse has always been a beacon of hope

My dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since the last time we spoke, and I have not once dared to write to you. But tonight, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars with fury, I feel the weight of time pressing upon me, and I know I must speak before the storm swallows me whole.

You were once my greatest joy, my light in the darkest of nights. I remember the days when you would climb the tower with me, wide-eyed and curious, asking endless questions about the sea and the stars. You would sit by the fire, listening as I told you stories of the ships that passed, of the sailors who came and went, of the loneliness that sometimes crept in like a shadow. You would smile and say, "Daddy, I’ll never be lonely, because I have you."

And I believed it. I believed it with all my soul. But the world is cruel, Eleanor. It does not care for the bonds we forge, nor for the love we hold. You left, and I did not know how to call you back. I thought if I waited, if I remained silent, you would come to me of your own accord. But you did not. And so I remained here, alone with the sea and the wind and the ghosts of the past.

I have not seen you in five years, and I do

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 12th, 1887**

**The Lighthouse, Isle of Skye**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now, not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the weight of a man who has spent years watching the sea from these cliffs, and who has come to know the silence better than the roar of the waves. You have not written to me in five years, and though I have not written to you, I have not forgotten. The sea does not forget, and neither does a father.

I do not know if you will read these words. I do not know if you will even care. But I must write, for the storm that is coming tonight has brought with it a reckoning—both for the sea and for the heart that has long since been left behind.

You were a child when you left. A child with fire in your eyes and a heart that did not know the weight of the world. You told me you were going to be a woman of your own making, and I, in my pride, allowed you to go. I told myself that I was doing what was right, that I was letting you grow. But I was wrong. I was a man who clung to the past, who believed that the sea would always be there, and that the world would always be kind. And I was wrong.

You were not the only

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a beast outside, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very stones of this lighthouse. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, here I am, writing to you as though you were still the child who once ran up the stairs to kiss my cheek before you left.

I do not know if I will survive this night, but I must try. The storm is fierce, and the light must be kept burning, for it is the only thing that keeps the sailors safe. I have always believed that the light is a promise—**a promise that even in the darkest hour, there is still a way forward.**

You left us, Eliza. I do not know why. You were once the light in my life, the daughter I held close, the one who made me believe that love could outlast the storm. But you walked away, and I followed, not in anger, but in sorrow. I have not spoken your name in years, not because I have forgotten you, but because I have been too afraid to say it aloud, for fear that the words would bring more pain than comfort.

I have watched the sea from this tower for many years, and I have seen the waves rise and fall, the tides come and go. But nothing has ever felt as relentless as the pain of your absence. I have tried

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit here in the dim glow of the oil lamp, its flickering light casting long shadows across the stone walls of this old lighthouse. The wind howls like a wounded beast, rattling the windows and the timbers of the tower. The sea roars with fury, as though it seeks to swallow the land whole. I’ve watched such storms before, but this one feels different—more relentless, more final. I fear I may not make it through the night.

I write this letter not with the hope that you will receive it, but with the hope that it will reach you. I know the distance between us is vast, both in miles and in years. Five years have passed since you left, and though time has worn the edges of my heart, it has not erased the pain of your absence.

I remember the last time we spoke. You were so angry, so full of resentment. You said I had abandoned you, that I had chosen this lighthouse over our family. I told you I had no choice. The sea is a cruel master, and it does not forgive. I have spent my life in service to it, and in doing so, I have lost more than I can ever explain.

I have no regrets about the life I’ve lived here. The lighthouse has been my home, my sanctuary, my burden. I have watched the sun rise and set for decades

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 14, 1897**

**Lighthouse, Northumberland, England**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee outside these ancient stone walls. The sea, as ever, is restless, and the storm that looms on the horizon threatens to consume us all. I do not know if I will survive the night, and if I do not, I beg you, my child, to forgive me. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I have not written, nor have I sent a single letter, not even one. I have let time and silence take root between us, and I am now paying the price for that silence.

I have always been a man of duty, of steadfastness, and of quiet endurance. I have spent my life upon this rock, watching the sea, tending to the beacon, and keeping the light burning for those who sail the dark waters. But I have not been a father, not truly. I have not been there for you when you were a child, nor have I been there for you as you grew. I have let the sea take my time, my heart, and my voice. I have let the world move on without me, and I have let you move on without me.

I do not know what has changed in you, Eleanor. I do not know if you still remember the days when we sat by the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit here by the window, the wind howling like a banshee, the sea roiling with a fury that makes my bones shake. The lamps are lit, the gears of the great machine groan in protest, and I can feel the storm coming — not just in the sky, but in my heart. I've been a lighthouse keeper for over thirty years, and I've never felt so alone as I do tonight.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, silent years. I've tried to write to you, to send letters, to speak your name into the wind, but the post office always says they've never received a reply. I've tried to believe you were too young, too lost, too angry to answer. But I know the truth. I know you were hurt. I know I was not there when you needed me most.

I remember the night you left. The storm was worse than this, the waves clawing at the cliffs like they were trying to pull the very earth apart. I was on the tower, keeping watch, and I heard your voice from below — sharp, desperate, filled with pain. I ran down, and there you were, standing at the edge of the cliff, your eyes blazing with something I can't name. I reached for you, but you turned and ran, and I watched you disappear into the storm, and I never saw you again.

I’ve tried to forgive you, Eleanor

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very earth. I have been a lighthouse keeper for thirty years, and never before has the storm seemed so close, so insistent, as it does tonight. I fear I may not see the dawn.

You have not written to me in five years, nor have I dared to send a single letter. The silence between us has grown like the sea—vast, unyielding, and full of storms of its own. I have often wondered if you ever read the letters I sent, or if they were lost in the mail, like so many others. I have tried to forget the last time we spoke, but the memory lingers like the scent of salt on the air.

I have always believed that a father's duty is to protect his children, to guide them with the light of his own understanding. I have done my best, Eleanor. I have stood guard through every tempest, every night, every day, in the hope that the light would guide you home. But you have not come. You have not asked for a single thing. You have not even written.

I do not know what has become of you, my daughter. Have you grown into a woman of strength and grace, or have you been broken by the weight of the world? I have no way

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 12th, 1889**

**The Keepers’ Cottage, White崖 Point**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the wails of the damned, and the sea roiling as if it would swallow the very earth beneath us. The storm has come at last, and I fear I may not see the dawn. But I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I fear I may never have the chance again.

You were a child when you left, Eleanor. A child with a heart full of fire and a spirit too proud to acknowledge the pain you caused. I remember the day you stormed out of our home, your eyes blazing with the same defiance that once made you my greatest pride. You said you would not be bound by the expectations of a man who would rather see you in a dimly lit parlor than out in the world. You were right, in your way. I was a fool to think I could keep you safe within the walls of this old house, to chain you to a life of quiet duty and unspoken sorrow.

But I did not mean to break you. I only meant to protect you.

I have not spoken to you since that day, and I have not written. I have been a man of silence, of duty, of the light that burns in the night. You were

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. The winds howl like the ghosts of the sea, and the sky is a canvas of darkness, heavy with the weight of the storm that now threatens to tear the very heart of the world asunder. I write to you from the lighthouse, the steadfast sentinel of these shores, where the sea and I have stood watch together for many years. But tonight, I do not feel the same certainty as before.

You were once my greatest joy, my light in the darkest hours. I remember the day you were born, the way the sea seemed to pause and whisper your name into the night. But time, as relentless as the tide, has brought us to this place—where we are separated by more than just distance, but by silence, by choices made in haste and hearts hardened by sorrow.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have often wondered if you still remember the sound of my voice, or if it has been swallowed by the waves that have carried our memories away. I have tried to write to you, to explain, to apologize, but the letters have never reached you, or they have returned with no address. I have come to accept that perhaps I have been the one who has turned away from you, and I regret it more than I can say.

Tonight, as I prepare for the storm, I find myself thinking

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a quill that trembles in my hand, and a heart that feels as if it has been worn down by time, wind, and the weight of silence. Five years have passed since you left this house, and yet the echoes of your laughter still linger in the stone walls, as if they remember you better than I do.

You were but a child when you last stood in this room, your curls bouncing as you raced up the stairs to see the sea. I had told you that the lighthouse was not just a tower of stone, but a guardian of souls, a beacon for those lost in the dark. You believed me, as children do. But I fear I failed you in more ways than one.

I have not spoken to you in this time, not because I do not care, but because I feared my words would not be enough. I feared that the love I held for you would not be enough. I have been a lighthouse keeper, not a father, and I have let the sea of my own pride and regret drown the light of my affection.

Tonight, a storm is coming—a storm like none I have seen in my years here. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very cliffs we stand upon. I have seen the sky darken with a fury that speaks of ruin. I do not know if I will see dawn.

If I do

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit by the flickering light of the lighthouse, the sea howling outside like a wounded beast. The wind whips through the cracks in the stone, and the waves crash against the rocks with a fury that makes my hands tremble. I've spent many a night here, watching the storm brew on the horizon, but never have I felt its weight so keenly as I do tonight.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not heard a word from you. I have tried to write, to send letters through the post, but they have never reached you. I have tried to call, to knock on your door, but you have never answered. I have tried to forgive, to forget, and to move on, but I have not been able to. You were my heart, Eleanor, and I have lost it.

I do not know if I will survive this storm. The sea is wild tonight, and the lighthouse has stood for many years, but even the strongest stones can crumble. I have always believed in the light, in its power to guide and protect, but I fear that tonight, the light may not be enough.

I have thought of you often, Eleanor. I have thought of the way you used to laugh, the way your eyes sparkled when you spoke of your dreams. You were a bright light in my life, and I have not been able to find another. I have tried

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know it may not be the kind of greeting you've grown accustomed to. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and in that time, the world has turned, and so have I. The sea, ever faithful, has been my constant companion, and it is to the sea that I now turn my thoughts.

Tonight, the skies darken with a tempest that promises to be fierce. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves, once gentle, now crash against the cliffs with a fury that stirs both fear and reverence in my heart. I have spent many a night watching the horizon, but tonight feels different. I feel the weight of the storm pressing upon me, and I am not certain I shall see the dawn.

You know, my dear, that I have always been a man of duty, of steadfastness. The lighthouse has been my life, my refuge, and my burden. I have tended its flame, not for glory, but for the safety of those who sail these waters. I have watched the stars, the moon, and the sun rise and set, and in their quiet constancy, I have found solace. Yet, there is a part of me that longs for the warmth of your presence, for the sound of your voice, for the memories we once shared.

I have often wondered if the distance between us is a result of my own

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. Five long years have passed since you last called me father, and yet the silence between us has never felt so loud. I have watched the sea from these cliffs, night after night, and I have thought of you more than I have thought of the stars.

The wind is rising now, a low, mournful moan that seems to echo the sorrow I have carried for so long. The waves are growing restless, and the sky, once a deep, brooding blue, is now a swirling mass of dark clouds, as if the heavens themselves are weeping. I have lit the lamp, as I have done every evening since the day you left, but tonight, I fear it may not be enough.

I have tried, Eliza, to be the man you needed me to be. I have tried to be the father, the guide, the voice of reason. But I have failed you. I failed to understand the fire that burned in your heart, the storm that you carried within you. I did not know how to reach you, how to hold you, how to stop the tide of your sorrow from sweeping you away.

I have thought often of the days when you used to run to me with your scraped knees and wet hair, when your laughter was the sound of the wind through the lantern. Now, that sound is gone, replaced by the hollow silence

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside, and the sea, that ever-faithful companion, roars with fury. I fear the night may claim me before dawn, and thus I must speak, for I know not what tomorrow will bring.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have passed since I last saw your face. I have not spoken of you to the others, nor have I dared to speak of you to myself. Yet here I am, pen in hand, writing to you as if the years have not yet stolen the right to call you my daughter.

I do not know if you will read this, nor do I know if you will believe the words that follow. But I write because I must. I write because I have spent too long in silence, and the silence has grown cold and unkind.

You left in haste, as you always did. I remember the night you went. The moon was full, and the sea was calm, but your eyes were dark with something I could not name. You said you could not stay, that you needed to find your own way, that I had never understood you. I did not argue, for I feared I would only make you leave faster. And so I let you go, as I have let so many things go—your laughter, your kindness,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you this evening, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars with fury, as if the very heavens are crying out in protest. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, in this moment, I feel the weight of silence heavier than any storm.

You were once my light, my guiding star, and now it seems that even the lighthouse cannot keep its beacon steady. I have spent these years watching the sea, praying for the calm, hoping for a sign, a word, a letter—anything that might bring me peace. But the sea has been cruel, and so too has time.

I do not know if I will see another dawn. The storm is upon us, and the waves grow taller, more relentless. The lantern, which has guided countless ships through the darkest nights, now trembles in its frame. I have always believed that the light must never falter, even when the world is at its darkest. But tonight, I fear that even the light may fail.

You were always the daughter of the lighthouse. You were the one who loved the sea, who found solace in the rhythm of the tides, who understood the beauty in the chaos. I see it in your eyes when you speak of the stars, when you tell me of the stories you write. You have always been my muse, my pride, my sorrow.

I have not written to you

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast outside the lighthouse, rattling the shutters and shaking the very bones of this old place. The sea is restless tonight, and I fear it may not be the storm that claims me, but the silence that has grown between us.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five long, lonely years. I've watched the sun rise and set, the moon wax and wane, and the tides ebb and flow, yet I've never seen a single day without the weight of your absence pressing down upon me. You were my light, my guiding star, and now I am left in the dark.

I know you do not wish to hear from me. I know you have every reason to be angry, to be hurt. And I do not blame you. I would have been angry too, had I been in your place. But I write this not to beg for your forgiveness, but to tell you the truth—because I fear I may not have the chance to speak it again.

You were not the only one who changed. I did too. I became the lighthouse keeper you once knew, a man who no longer speaks, who no longer listens, who no longer remembers how to love. But I still remember you, Eleanor. I remember the way your laughter echoed through these stone walls, the way your eyes sparkled like the sea at dawn,

**The Lighthouse, Isle of Skye**  
**April 15th, 1889**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the wails of the damned, and the sea roiling as though it seeks to swallow the very cliffs that hold us. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may not be kind to this old frame. Yet I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five long years, and I would not leave this world without at least one final word.

You were always the light in my life, my dear Eleanor. I remember the day you were born—your cries like bells in the night, and your eyes, so wide and full of wonder. I used to think you would grow up to be a great lady, a queen of the world, and I would be but a humble keeper, watching the sea from a tower. But time, as it often does, has a cruel way of turning dreams into shadows.

I do not know if you remember the last time we spoke. It was a bitter winter, and I had been reprimanded for allowing a young sailor to take shelter in the lighthouse. You wrote me a letter, sharp and cold, saying that I had no right to care for others when I had no care for my own family. I did not reply. I could not. The words would not come. And so we fell into silence

**Dear Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this lighthouse, and the sea roiling as if it would swallow the world whole. The storm is coming—dark, furious, and I fear it may take more than the sea from us.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years since you walked out of this door, your back turned, your voice cold as the salt air. I have not spoken to you since, and yet I have not forgotten. Every night, I see your face in the flickering light of the beacon, and I wonder if you ever think of me at all.

You were a child when you left, Eleanor. A child who believed the world was a place of endless possibility, of grand dreams and distant horizons. But the world is not so kind. It does not bend to the whims of youth. It demands strength, and I have not given you that strength. I have given you only silence.

I have kept this lighthouse for years now, not for glory, but to keep the sea from claiming the land. It is my duty, my burden, my life. I have watched the tides rise and fall, the stars blink out one by one, and the sun set behind the clouds like a dying ember. But I have never seen the sun rise again without thinking of you.

I do not know if I will survive this storm. I do not know if I

My Dearest Eliza,

If you are reading these words, then perhaps I have not yet succumbed to the elements that now rage upon this coast, or perhaps you have already heard of the storm that has descended upon us with such fury. I write this in the dim glow of the lighthouse, the wind howling like the very devil himself, and the sea, that ancient and ever-watchful thing, lashing at the cliffs with a vengeance.

Five years have passed since you left this place, and yet the weight of your absence lingers in the air, thick as the smoke that curls from the hearth. I have tried, in these long, lonely nights, to forget the last time we spoke, the way your eyes seemed to harden when you told me you were leaving, that you could not bear to see me any longer. I have tried to forgive you, and yet I find that the wound remains, deep and festering, like a scar that refuses to fade.

I have not spoken of you to the other keepers, nor have I mentioned your name to the few visitors who come to see the lighthouse. I have kept your memory locked away in the same chest where I keep the letters you once sent, now yellowed and brittle with age. I have not opened them in years, for I fear what I might find within.

But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in a long while.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it finds me in a state of unrest. The sea is restless tonight, and the wind howls like a banshee across the cliffs. I have not slept in days, and the lantern’s beam flickers with the same uncertainty I feel in my own soul. It is not the storm that troubles me most, but the silence between us.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not heard a word from you. I have tried to write, to send letters, to send messages through the postman who comes once a fortnight. But they have always returned unopened, or with a note that said only, *“Not delivered.”* I have long since stopped asking why. **Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. It has been five long years since you left, and though I have tried to move on, the weight of your absence has never lifted. Tonight, the sky is dark and the winds howl like the wails of the sea, and I fear I may not see another dawn. But if I do, I must tell you this—**I have never stopped loving you.**

I remember the day you left, Eleanor. It was a quiet morning, the kind that feels too still for such a storm. You stood at the door, your eyes filled with a sorrow I could not understand, and you said nothing. I begged you to stay, but you turned away, and I could not chase you. You were gone before I could even speak your name. I have not spoken it since.

I know you must have thought I was cruel, that I had no right to ask you to stay. But I was not cruel. I was broken. I had lost my wife, my purpose, my reason to live, and I had no idea how to mend the pieces. I thought that by staying here, in this lighthouse, I might find some solace. But the sea does not forgive, and neither does the heart.

You were always the light in my life, Eleanor. You were the reason I kept the beacon burning
